lesson.menu_desc: Recommend and decide in a meeting: should and ought to for advice, and had better for strong advice with a consequence -- weigh the options, the pros and cons, and reach a consensus -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- Decision-Making -- I Suggest We...
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Claudia Curimbaba | Aula 18 -- Decision-Making

listenings.text: Good morning. Before the decision meeting, let me give you my honest read on the three options. Option one is to expand the Houston plant now. The upside is speed; we should capture the demand while it is high. The downside is the cost, so we had better not commit the full budget in a single quarter. Option two is to postpone the expansion and invest in the existing line first. It is the safe path, and I think we ought to consider it seriously, because it protects our margins. The trade-off is that we move slower than the competition. Option three is a smaller pilot project. It limits the risk, but the return is modest. My recommendation is simple. We should weigh the pros and cons of each path against our cash position, we ought to prioritize the option that protects the partnership, and we had better decide today, because the supplier needs an answer by Friday. Whatever we choose, let us reach a consensus before the call with David, so we speak with one voice.

Before I lead the decision meeting, I want my language to be clear and confident. When I give normal advice, I will say we should review the numbers, or we ought to consider the alternative. Should and ought to have the same meaning here; ought to is just a little more formal. When the advice is strong and there is a real consequence, I will use had better. I had better warn the team that we have a deadline, and we had better not delay, or we lose the contract. Had better is not about the past; it is about now and the near future, and it always carries a small warning. I also want to frame each option fairly, so I will name the upside and the downside, the pros and the cons, and the trade-off for each path. My job is not to decide alone. My job is to weigh the options out loud, recommend a direction, and help the team reach a consensus. If I separate normal advice from strong advice, and if I present each alternative with its pros and cons, the decision will be sound and everyone will own it.

extra_audio.text: First, you state the decision clearly, so everyone knows what the meeting must resolve. Then, you lay out the options on the table, two or three realistic alternatives, not more. After that, you weigh the pros and cons of each one, the upside, the downside, and the trade-off. Next, you make a recommendation with should or ought to, and you flag any strong warning with had better. Finally, you invite the team to reach a consensus and you confirm the next step, so the decision becomes an action.
